---
title: "The Rule, Section by Section"
---

This is a plain-language guide to what the proposed rule changes, section by section.
Use the search box (top right) to find a section, or jump to the changes most likely to
affect your work. Each comment you file should **begin with the section number in
brackets**, e.g. `[200.205]` — so we list that number for every entry.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**How to read the tiers.**
[critical]{.tier .tier-critical} = directly reshapes how science is funded, governed, or
communicated. [notable]{.tier .tier-notable} = meaningful change worth a comment if it
touches you. [minor]{.tier .tier-minor} = largely technical or housekeeping
(e.g., renaming "guidance" to "regulation").

Quotations below are from the proposed rule's own preamble. The full text is in
[Docket OMB-2026-0034](https://www.regulations.gov/docket/OMB-2026-0034).
:::

---

## The structural change behind everything {#guidance-to-regulation}

[Parts 1, 180, 182, 183]{.secnum} [critical]{.tier .tier-critical}

OMB proposes to replace the word **"guidance" with "regulation"** throughout 2 CFR and to
delete sentences like *"is guidance not regulation"* from §180.15 and §182.15. This converts
decades of widely-followed *guidance* into **binding regulation**. The practical effect:
future administrations can change the rules governing all federal grants with less — or no —
public notice-and-comment, and agencies "must not deviate" from the text. It consolidates the
entire federal grantmaking enterprise into one hard-to-amend regulation.

**Why it matters for science:** this is the mechanism that makes every other change below
durable and removes the public's future voice. **Talking point:** the public comment process
itself is at stake; converting guidance to binding regulation should require the fullest, not the
narrowest, public deliberation.

---

## Awarding grants: politics over peer review

### §200.205 — Federal agency review of merit of proposals {#s200-205}

[200.205]{.secnum} [critical]{.tier .tier-critical}

Establishes a **new pre-issuance review by senior political appointees**. Per the rule, these
appointees must apply principles "ensuring that discretionary awards advance the President's
policy priorities," and it states plainly that **"peer review remains advisory and does not
replace agency discretion."** Agencies are "not required to issue awards solely as a result of
issuing a NOFO."

**Why it matters for science:** for ~80 years, federal science funding has been decided
primarily by independent expert review of scientific merit. This subordinates that review to
political judgment about Presidential priorities. **Talking point:** describe what merit review
means in your field and what is lost when a political appointee can override it.

### §200.206 — Federal agency review of risk posed by applicants {#s200-206}

[200.206]{.secnum} [notable]{.tier .tier-notable}

Expands the "risk" factors an agency may weigh before funding, including an applicant's
"affiliations with organizations engaged in activities that violate Federal law, undermine public
safety or national security, or advocate for the overthrow of the United States Government," and
compliance with foreign gift/contract disclosure (Section 117 of the Higher Education Act).

**Why it matters:** broad, vaguely-defined "affiliation" and "questionable practices" factors
invite viewpoint- or association-based screening of applicants and institutions.

---

## New ideological prohibitions

### §200.218 — Prohibition of theories of disparate-impact liability {#s200-218}

[200.218]{.secnum} [critical]{.tier .tier-critical}

A **new section** barring recipients from using federal awards to "promote or support theories
that impose disparate-impact liability based on federally protected characteristics such as race,
sex, or age," tied to Executive Order 14281.

**Why it matters for science:** disparate-impact analysis is a standard, decades-old method
across public health, epidemiology, social science, economics, and education research. A
prohibition on "promoting or supporting" such "theories" with federal funds could chill or
defund entire legitimate fields of study. **Talking point:** name the established research in
your field that uses disparate-impact analysis and what would be lost.

### §200.219 — Prohibition of discriminatory event services {#s200-219}

[200.219]{.secnum} [notable]{.tier .tier-notable}

A **new section** requiring recipients (including public universities) not to discriminate on
"viewpoint, content, or subject matter of speech" when providing services for events — framed
around so-called "heckler's fees" for security at speaker events. Applies even to events not
directly funded by the award if held on the recipient's property.

**Why it matters:** extends federal control into institutions' management of campus events and
facilities, with compliance risk attached to all federal funding.

### §200.220 — Prohibition of covered foreign collaborations {#s200-220}

[200.220]{.secnum} [critical]{.tier .tier-critical}

A **new section** extending Wolf-Amendment-style restrictions **government-wide**, prohibiting
use of federal funds for "bilateral or multilateral collaborations… with covered foreign countries
or covered foreign entities" unless an agency head grants an exception. It applies to "research,
technical assistance, travel, or indirect costs allocable to such collaborations."

**Why it matters for science:** international collaboration is fundamental to modern research.
A broad, government-wide default prohibition (previously limited to specific agencies like NASA)
could disrupt co-authorships, data sharing, conferences, and student/postdoc exchanges across
many fields. **Talking point:** describe your international collaborations and what a default ban
plus case-by-case agency approval would do to them.

---

## Killing grants already underway

### §200.340 — Termination and suspension {#s200-340}

[200.340]{.secnum} [critical]{.tier .tier-critical}

The single largest change in the rule. It clarifies and expands **discretionary termination**,
codifying Executive Order 14332: an agency "may terminate a Federal award in part or its
entirety if the Federal agency… determines that a termination is in the interest of the Federal
agency," including when an award "no longer advances agency priorities or the national interest."
The rule explicitly contemplates that priorities "may change in response to new direction from
politically accountable leadership," and adds new **temporary suspension** authority.

**Why it matters for science:** multi-year research depends on funding stability. The ability to
end active awards mid-stream for shifting political "priorities" puts every ongoing project,
trainee, and clinical study at risk and aligns grant management with at-will contract
cancellation. **Talking point:** explain what happens to your people, data, subjects, or
equipment if a 5-year award is cut in year 2 "for convenience."

### §200.341–200.343 — Notification, objections, and effects of termination {#s200-341}

[200.341–.343]{.secnum} [notable]{.tier .tier-notable}

Govern how terminations are noticed, how recipients may object/appeal, and what costs are
allowable after termination. Worth reading alongside §200.340 if termination risk affects you.

---

## Restricting scientific communication

### §200.432 — Conferences {#s200-432}

[200.432]{.secnum} [critical]{.tier .tier-critical}

Makes conference **attendance** costs "allowable only if participation in the conference is
expressly approved by the agency and included in the terms and conditions of the award."

**Why it matters for science:** presenting and attending conferences is core to disseminating
results, training students, and building collaborations. Requiring case-by-case agency
pre-approval adds friction and a political gate to ordinary scientific exchange.

### §200.461 — Publication and printing costs {#s200-461}

[200.461]{.secnum} [critical]{.tier .tier-critical}

Makes publication costs "unallowable unless such costs are expressly required by statute or
approved in advance by the Federal agency on a case-by-case basis," reasoning that "publication
costs are not inherently necessary to carry out the core programmatic objectives of most Federal
awards."

**Why it matters for science:** publishing *is* the output of research. This sweeps in
article processing charges and open-access fees, directly conflicting with federal open-access
policy and making it harder to share federally funded results. **Talking point:** state your
typical publication/APC costs and why publishing is integral, not "ancillary," to your grant.

### §200.477 — Abortion {#s200-477}

[200.477]{.secnum} [notable]{.tier .tier-notable}

New section making costs associated with elective abortions unallowable except as authorized by
federal law (codifying existing Hyde-Amendment-type restrictions as a cost principle).

---

## Other changes worth knowing

| Section | Tier | What changes |
|---|---|---|
| [200.216]{.secnum} — Prohibited equipment/services | [notable]{.tier .tier-notable} | Expands prohibitions on certain foreign equipment, services, and systems. |
| [200.300]{.secnum} — Statutory & national policy requirements | [critical]{.tier .tier-critical} | Houses the **unlawful-DEI provision** and the legal-authority analysis for §200.218. |
| [200.303]{.secnum} — Internal controls | [notable]{.tier .tier-notable} | Revised internal-control and information-security expectations. |
| [200.322]{.secnum} — Domestic preferences | [notable]{.tier .tier-notable} | Strengthened "Buy American"-style procurement preferences. |
| [200.503]{.secnum} — Audit requirements | [minor]{.tier .tier-minor} | Limits added audits to those authorized by statute. |
| Parts 25, 170, 175 | [minor]{.tier .tier-minor} | Mostly "guidance"→"regulation" wording; FSRS.gov references updated to SAM.gov. |
| Part 176 (ARRA) | [minor]{.tier .tier-minor} | Removed — no longer needed. |

---

*Working on your comment? See [How to Comment](../how-to-comment.qmd) for the process, or
[Draft with AI](../draft-with-ai.qmd) to turn your situation into a first draft.*
